Document [0] (Title: Pediatric Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Glucocorticoids) As with outpatient therapy, clinicians should administer glucocorticoids promptly to children with moderate-to-severe asthma exacerbations, as their early administration reduces hospital admission rates. Oral and IV glucocorticoids have equivalent effects when given in comparable doses. [46] OCS are equally efficacious and preferred due to their less invasive nature. Dexamethasone, at 0.6 mg/kg/d for 2 days, is often preferred in the emergency department due to its long half-life and equivalent efficacy to prednisone. [47] [48] When administering prednisone or prednisolone, the dosage is 1 to 2 mg/kg/d with a maximum dosage of 20 mg/d in children aged 0 to 2, 30 mg/d in children aged 3 to 5, and 40 mg/d in children aged 6 to 11 for 3 to 5 days. A shorter course and lower dose of OCS are equally as effective as a higher dose and longer course. IV steroids are necessary for patients with impending or actual respiratory arrest or those who are intolerant of oral glucocorticoids.

The strategy of initiating ICS and PRN SABA decreases asthma exacerbations and reduces the use of systemic corticosteroids. Step 2: Daily low-dose ICS (inhaled corticosteroids) as controller therapy and PRN SABA as quick relief therapy. An alternative regimen is daily montelukast or cromolyn and PRN SABA. The alternative regimen is a daily medium dose of ICS + montelukast and PRN SABA. Step 5: Daily high-dose ICS-LABA as controller therapy and PRN SABA for quick relief therapy. The alternative regimen is a daily high dose of ICS + montelukast and PRN SABA.

Document [13] (Title: Inhaled Corticosteroids -- Continuing Education Activity) Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) are the FDA-indicated treatment of choice in preventing asthma exacerbation in patients with persistent asthma. Persistent asthma is classified by symptoms more than two days a week, more than three nighttime awakenings per month, more than twice a week using short-acting beta-2 agonists for symptom control, or any limitation of normal activity due to asthma. Regular use of these medications reduces the frequency of asthma symptoms, bronchial hyper-responsiveness, risk of serious exacerbation, and improves the quality of life. This activity describes the mode of action of inhaled corticosteroids, including mechanism of action, pharmacology, adverse event profiles, eligible patient populations, monitoring, and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the management of these patients.

This category includes Momesalic, budesonid, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate, and it has child entities representing related steroid formulations such as dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate (the latter being a specific glucocorticoid inhalant within the same therapeutic group). Collectively, these relationships illustrate that specific corticosteroids serve multiple therapeutic roles: as standalone nasal preparations, as components of β‑adrenergic combination inhalers, and as primary glucocorticoid inhalants for lower‑airway obstructive diseases, providing clinicians with versatile options for managing both upper and lower airway inflammation.

Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 424) Adrenergic inhalants that are combined with corticosteroids (excluding anticholinergics) constitute a therapeutic class that includes several fixed‑dose combination products such as albuterol‑beclomethasone, budesonide/formoterol, formoterol/mometasone, and vilanterol/fluticasone furoate. Within these combinations, the corticosteroid components are represented by budesonide (contained in budesonide/formoterol), mometasone (contained in formoterol/mometasone), and fluticasone furoate (a component of vilanterol/fluticasone furoate). Corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use comprise the same corticosteroid agents—mometasone (often referred to as Momesalic), budesonide, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate—highlighting their shared role in delivering anti‑inflammatory therapy to the nasal mucosa. Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease also list these five agents (mometasone, budesonide, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate) as members, indicating that they are the primary inhaled steroids used to manage conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. This inhaled glucocorticoid class is hierarchically linked to other glucocorticoids: it has prednisolone sodium succinate and triamcinolone diacetate as child entities, while triamcinolonacetonid is identified as a parent entity, reflecting the chemical and pharmacologic relationships among these steroids. Together, these interrelated categories illustrate how specific corticosteroid agents are employed across different delivery routes (nasal, inhaled) and formulations (single agents versus adrenergic‑corticosteroid combinations) to treat inflammatory airway diseases.
